text: So this video is going to describe a robotic pleural decortication, which is an operation I always used to do using vats. But since I started using the da Vinci, I found that I've had significantly better results. The patient is a young woman with a left-sided empyema. She had had a pigtail chest tube placed by the interventional radiologist and had intrapleural fibrinolytic protocol, but hadn't really responded. So we elected to take her to surgery. The key to this operation is the setup. You can see the first incision is that most medial incision where we put an alexis wound protector and we bluntly dissect out the space and then get three eight millimeter ports with a thirty degree down camera and then we put the air seal twelve millimeter system port right through the alexis so we can inject the insufflation. Here's a video where I'm actually showing how we do it. So you can see the most medial port. This is the sixth inner space. It's kind of like the standard VATS incision. And I'm going to get in and sort of bluntly dissect a space with my fingers. So I know the diaphragm's down and I have a little bit of room. And then I take this Alexis wound protector, the extra small size, and we put it in there and just kind of roll it down like a turtleneck. And we can put the thirty degree camera right through the Alexis. We'll suck out some fluid and we'll get the camera and ring clamp. And you can see I'm sort of dissecting the diaphragm away. And then under direct vision,

The surgeon inserts three eight-millimeter robotic ports under direct vision, positioning them above the diaphragm. The setup includes a long bipolar grasper in the right hand, a thirty-degree camera in the middle port, and a force bipolar in the left hand. The air seal system is introduced through the Alexis wound protector to insufflate the space, pushing the diaphragm down. The pericardium is visible on the left side of the screen, with the lower lobe adhered to the chest wall. Sponge rolls are used for blunt dissection between the lung and chest wall.

The surgeon uses a sponge roll to perform blunt dissection, separating the lung from the chest wall. The assistant uses suction to aid in mobilizing the lung, while the surgeon strips away the thick pleural peel using the force bipolar in the left hand.

The surgeon mobilizes the lung away from the pericardium and begins to divide the inferior pulmonary ligament for increased lung mobility. The major fissure is identified and separated, revealing a thin fibrous peel on the lung surface. The surgeon carefully strips off sections of the peel, following its leading edge to remove it.

The surgeon uses a sponge roll, resembling a Kittner dissector, to remove pleural peel from the lung surface, ensuring the lower lobe can expand fully. The force bipolar is set to a firm grip to carefully strip the peel without damaging the lung parenchyma, avoiding potential air leaks.

The surgeon inspects the medial side of the lung, ensuring it is not adhered to surrounding structures. The major fissure is visibly opened, and the assistant uses a suction device to clear fluid and a grasper to manipulate sponge rolls. A small amount of fibrin is observed at the apex, which is mostly removed, while the diaphragm is mobilized.

The surgeon pauses the air seal and the anesthesiologist inflates the lung, which visibly expands to fill the chest cavity. Two chest tubes are then placed: a 28 or 32 French apical chest tube extending posteriorly to the apex, and a right-angle tube along the diaphragm.

This patient had a nice result. This was the x-ray before, immediately before we did surgery with the pigtail catheter in there. And then this is the chest x-ray at her post-operative visit a week after discharge, maybe ten days after surgery. And she had a great result. So that's a robotic decortication. Thanks for watching and contact me if you have any questions.
